s: In the Book of Revelation, the Beast from the Sea has seven heads, ten horns, and combines features of a leopard, bear, and lion — and its number, 666, may actually be a coded reference to Emperor Nero using Hebrew numerology (gematria).
q: The number 666 in the Book of Revelation is thought by many scholars to be a coded reference to which Roman Emperor?
e: When 'Nero Caesar' is transliterated into Hebrew letters and their numerical values added, the total is 666. Some early manuscripts even give the number as 616, which corresponds to the Latin spelling of Nero's name.

s: Sha Wujing (Sandy) from Journey to the West was originally a celestial general who was banished to Earth for breaking the Jade Emperor's favorite crystal goblet — he became a river ogre who wore a necklace of nine skulls from previous failed monks.
q: Why was Sha Wujing (Sandy) banished from heaven in Journey to the West?
a: He broke the Jade Emperor's crystal goblet
d: For breaking the Jade Emperor's palace
For losing a battle on Earth
e: The nine skulls belonged to nine reincarnations of the very monk (Xuanzang) he later swore to protect. When Sandy tried to cross the river with the skulls, they sank — except these nine, which floated, hinting at their sacred nature.

s: The White Stag appears across Celtic, Hungarian, and Arthurian mythology as an uncatchable creature that leads heroes on quests — in every tradition, the chase itself matters more than the capture, because the stag can never truly be caught.
q: What does the White Stag represent across multiple mythological traditions?
a: An uncatchable creature that leads heroes on quests
e: The White Stag symbolizes the pursuit of spiritual knowledge or a divine quest. In Hungarian legend, it led the brothers Hunor and Magor to their new homeland. In Arthurian romance, it triggered quests for the Holy Grail.

s: Hebe, the Greek goddess of youth, served as the cupbearer to the gods on Olympus, pouring their nectar and ambrosia — she was fired from this job when she tripped and fell in an embarrassing way, and was replaced by the mortal boy Ganymede.
q: Which Greek goddess was replaced as cupbearer to the gods by the mortal Ganymede?
e: Zeus was so taken with the beautiful mortal Ganymede that he abducted him (in eagle form) and gave him Hebe's old job, granting him immortality. The constellation Aquarius ('water-bearer') may commemorate Ganymede.

s: The Yilbegan in Turkic mythology is a multi-headed dragon that can regrow its severed heads — the hero must find and destroy a hidden life-force (often stored in an egg inside a duck inside a hare inside a chest) to truly kill it.
q: What makes the Yilbegan of Turkic mythology nearly impossible to kill?
a: Its life-force is hidden in a nested container (egg inside duck inside hare inside chest)
d: It can fly higher than any archer
It grows stronger as it fights
Its roar turns enemies to stone
It can become invisible at will
e: This 'nested soul' motif appears across Eurasian folklore, from Russian Koschei the Deathless to the Yilbegan. The idea that a monster's weakness is hidden in an impossibly nested container is one of mythology's most widespread patterns.

s: The Longana is an aquatic dragon-serpent in Italian Alpine folklore that lives in mountain lakes of the Dolomites — it can shapeshift into a beautiful woman to lure travelers, and its tears are said to have created several Alpine lakes.
q: The Longana of Italian Alpine folklore is said to have created Alpine lakes from what?
e: The Longana stories are unique because they place a dragon tradition in the European Alps rather than the typical medieval lowland settings. Each Dolomite valley has its own version of which lake the Longana's tears created.

s: In Norse mythology, the squirrel Ratatoskr runs up and down the world tree Yggdrasil carrying insults between the eagle at the top and the dragon Nidhogg at the roots — he is essentially the world's first troll, stirring up conflict for entertainment.
q: What does the squirrel Ratatoskr do in Norse mythology?
a: Carries insults between the eagle and dragon on the world tree
e: Ratatoskr serves no cosmic purpose other than keeping two powerful beings angry at each other. Some scholars interpret him as an ancient metaphor for gossip and the destructive power of words.

s: In Japanese mythology, the thunder god Raijin has a ring of drums floating behind him that he beats to create thunder — his companion Fujin, the wind god, carries a bag of winds, and together they were so terrifying that the 'divine wind' (kamikaze) was attributed to them.
q: The Japanese term 'kamikaze' (divine wind) was originally attributed to which pair of gods?
a: Raijin and Fujin (thunder and wind gods)
e: When a typhoon destroyed the invading Mongol fleet in 1281, the Japanese credited Raijin and Fujin with sending the 'divine wind.' This event was so culturally significant that the term 'kamikaze' was later adopted for WWII suicide pilots.

s: The Aboriginal Dreamtime is not just mythology — it is a living navigational system. Songlines encode precise geographic directions, water sources, and seasonal food availability into songs that can guide travelers across hundreds of miles of featureless desert.
q: In Aboriginal Australian culture, what practical function do Dreamtime songlines serve?
a: They encode navigational directions and survival information into songs
e: A person who knows the right songline can cross Australia by singing the landscape into existence, recognizing features described in ancestral songs. This is arguably the oldest functioning information system on Earth, predating writing by tens of thousands of years.

s: In Aztec mythology, Quetzalcoatl (the Feathered Serpent) descended to the underworld to steal the bones of the previous humanity from the death god Mictlantecuhtli — he tripped and broke them, which is why humans come in different sizes.
q: According to Aztec mythology, why do humans come in different sizes?
a: Quetzalcoatl tripped and broke the bones he was using to create them
d: He swallowed them and they broke inside him
He dropped them from the sky
He couldn't carry all of them
He was cursed by the gods
He burned them in sacred fire
e: This creation myth explains human diversity through divine clumsiness. Quetzalcoatl reassembled the broken bones and sprinkled them with his own blood to create the current generation of humans.

s: In Vodou (Voodoo), Baron Samedi is the lord of the dead who wears a top hat, tuxedo, and dark glasses, and speaks in a nasal voice — he stands at the crossroads between life and death, and no one can die unless he digs their grave.
q: In Vodou, what must Baron Samedi do before anyone can die?
e: Baron Samedi is one of the most charismatic figures in any mythology — he drinks rum, smokes cigars, makes crude jokes, and is utterly irreverent despite holding the ultimate power over life and death. He is also a protector of children.

s: The Anansi spider stories originated with the Ashanti people of Ghana but survived the Atlantic slave trade largely intact — enslaved Africans carried these tales to the Caribbean, where Anansi became a symbol of using wit to overcome oppression.
q: Anansi spider stories originated with which African people?
e: Anansi is one of the most important figures in the African diaspora. His stories of a small, clever spider outwitting stronger opponents became coded lessons in resistance and survival during slavery.

s: In Mesopotamian mythology, Tiamat was a primordial sea goddess whose body was split in half by the god Marduk to create the heavens and the earth — her eyes became the sources of the Tigris and Euphrates rivers.
q: In Mesopotamian mythology, which god split Tiamat's body to create heaven and earth?
e: The Enuma Elish creation epic (c. 1100 BCE) describes Tiamat as a dragon-like being of saltwater chaos. Her defeat by Marduk established the cosmic order and made Babylon's patron god supreme among the Mesopotamian pantheon.

s: The Pooka (Puca) of Irish folklore is a shapeshifter that most commonly appears as a sleek black horse — if you ride it, it takes you on a terrifying midnight gallop and dumps you in a ditch at dawn, but it never actually kills anyone.
q: In Irish folklore, what form does the Pooka most commonly take?
e: The Pooka is one of Ireland's most nuanced supernatural beings — not evil, not good, just chaotic. Farmers left a share of their harvest called the 'Pooka's share' in the fields to stay on its good side.

s: In Maori mythology, the Sky Father Ranginui and Earth Mother Papatuanuku were locked in a tight embrace, leaving their children trapped in darkness between them — the god Tane finally pushed them apart to create daylight, and their tears of separation became rain and mist.
q: In Maori mythology, rain and mist are said to come from whose tears?
a: The separated Sky Father and Earth Mother
e: This creation myth beautifully explains both the separation of sky and earth and the origin of weather. The other children had different plans — Tu wanted to kill their parents, while Tawhirimatea (storm god) sided with them and has been raging ever since.

s: Baba Yaga, the witch of Slavic folklore, lives in a hut that walks around on giant chicken legs and is surrounded by a fence made of human bones topped with glowing skulls — yet she sometimes helps the hero rather than eating them, depending on whether they show proper manners.
q: What is unusual about Baba Yaga's house in Slavic folklore?
a: It walks around on giant chicken legs
d: It rotates on a stone pillar
It sinks into the ground at night
e: Baba Yaga is one of mythology's most complex figures — neither purely good nor evil. She tests visitors with impossible tasks and either helps or devours them based on their politeness, cleverness, and moral worth.

s: In Egyptian mythology, the goddess Nut swallowed the sun every evening and gave birth to it every morning — her body, arched across the sky covered in stars, WAS the sky itself, and Ra traveled through her body each night.
q: In Egyptian mythology, how did the goddess Nut cause the daily cycle of the sun?
a: She swallowed it each evening and gave birth to it each morning
d: She carried it on her back
She held it in her hands
She kept it in a sacred vessel
She breathed it out and in
She pushed it across the sky with her feet
e: Nut was literally the canopy of the heavens. Her husband Geb (the Earth) lies beneath her, and the air god Shu stands between them holding her up. Ancient Egyptian coffin lids often depicted Nut on the inside, protecting the dead with her starry body.

s: In Irish folklore, the Clurichaun is a perpetually drunk fairy cousin of the Leprechaun who haunts wine cellars and will wreck your house if you try to move him.
q: In Irish folklore, what fairy creature haunts wine cellars and is always drunk?
e: While Leprechauns guard gold, Clurichauns guard — and enthusiastically drink — household liquor. Homeowners who tried relocating them found the Clurichaun had followed, hidden in a barrel.

s: The Vegetable Lamb of Tartary was a medieval European legend about a plant that grew tiny sheep as its fruit, with stems attached to their navels.
q: What medieval legend described a plant that grew tiny living sheep as fruit?
e: Medieval travelers to Central Asia reported a zoophyte — half plant, half animal — that produced wool-bearing lambs on stalks. Scholars now believe the legend arose from misidentified cotton plants.

s: Karkinos was a giant crab in Greek mythology sent by Hera to distract Heracles during his fight with the Hydra — Heracles simply crushed it underfoot.
q: What giant crab did Hera send to fight Heracles during his battle with the Hydra?
e: Despite its inglorious death, Hera honored the crab by placing it among the stars as the constellation Cancer. It is one of mythology's most anticlimactic battle participants.

s: The Makara in Hindu mythology is a sea creature hybrid that serves as the mount of the river goddess Ganga and appears on nearly every Hindu temple gateway.
q: What hybrid sea creature from Hindu mythology serves as a mount for the goddess Ganga?
e: Part crocodile, part elephant, part fish — the Makara's exact form varies by region. It symbolizes the threshold between the earthly and divine, which is why it decorates temple entrances as a protective guardian.

s: The Olmec civilization believed in werejaguars — humans who could transform into jaguars — and their art depicts babies with jaguar fangs and snarling mouths.
q: Which ancient Mesoamerican civilization created mythology and art depicting humans who transform into jaguars?
e: The Olmec werejaguar is one of Mesoamerica's oldest mythological motifs, dating to roughly 1500 BCE. The eerie half-human, half-jaguar infant figures are among the most distinctive art of any ancient culture.

s: Zhu Bajie from Journey to the West was originally a heavenly marshal who got drunk at a banquet and flirted with the Moon Goddess, so he was reincarnated as a pig.
q: In Journey to the West, why was Zhu Bajie (Pigsy) turned into a pig?
a: He drunkenly flirted with the Moon Goddess
d: He tried to steal divine treasures
He refused to obey the Jade Emperor
He married a celestial being secretly
e: Often called Pigsy in English, Zhu Bajie is one of Chinese literature's most beloved characters. Despite his gluttony and laziness, he remains loyal to the monk Tripitaka throughout their pilgrimage.

s: In Greek mythology, the Hecatoncheires were three giants with 100 arms and 50 heads each — and they were Zeus's secret weapon in the war against the Titans.
q: How many arms did each of the Hecatoncheires have in Greek mythology?
e: Named Briareus, Cottus, and Gyges, these beings were so terrifying that even their father Uranus imprisoned them underground. Zeus freed them, and their ability to hurl 300 boulders simultaneously turned the tide of the Titanomachy.

s: The Drekavac is a South Slavic monster said to be the soul of an unbaptized child — its name literally means 'the screamer' because its piercing shriek predicts death.
q: What South Slavic creature is believed to be the screaming soul of an unbaptized child?
e: In Serbian and Bosnian folklore, the Drekavac appears at night near cemeteries and waterways. Its form varies from a stretched-out child to a bird-like creature, but its blood-curdling scream is the constant feature.

s: Charon, the ferryman of the Greek underworld, charged exactly one obol coin — which is why ancient Greeks placed coins on the eyes or in the mouths of the dead.
q: Why did ancient Greeks place coins on their dead?
a: To pay Charon the ferryman to cross the River Styx
d: To guarantee safe passage through the afterlife
To appease the river god Charon
To feed Cerberus, the three-headed dog
To unlock the gates of Hades
To pay taxes to the underworld kingdom
Because it was currency in ancient Greece
e: Those who could not pay Charon were condemned to wander the shores of the River Styx for 100 years. This funeral custom persisted across the Mediterranean for centuries.

s: The Baku in Japanese mythology is a dream-eating chimera with an elephant's trunk, tiger's paws, and an ox's tail — and children were taught to call on it after nightmares.
q: What Japanese mythological creature eats nightmares when children call its name?
e: After a bad dream, Japanese children traditionally say 'Baku-san, come eat my dream!' The creature devours the nightmare, but legends warn that a too-hungry Baku might eat your hopes and ambitions too.

s: The bunyip of Aboriginal Australian mythology was so feared that the word was used by early colonists to mean 'imposter' or 'fraud' — a meaning still used in Australian English.
q: What Aboriginal Australian mythological creature's name became an Australian English word meaning 'imposter'?
e: Descriptions of the bunyip vary wildly across Aboriginal nations — from a giant starfish to a river horse. Some researchers believe bunyip legends may preserve folk memories of real extinct megafauna like Diprotodon.

s: The White Stag appears across European mythology from Hungary to Britain — chasing it always leads the hunter to a life-changing encounter or a new kingdom.
q: In European mythology, what animal leads hunters on a chase that always ends in a life-changing encounter?
e: In Hungarian legend, the White Stag led brothers Hunor and Magor to their new homeland. In Arthurian legend, pursuing it leads to encounters with fairy queens. The stag is never actually caught.

s: The táltos in Hungarian mythology is born with teeth or extra fingers, and gains shamanic powers only after spending three days in a trance fighting other táltos in spirit form.
q: In Hungarian mythology, what must a táltos do to gain shamanic powers?
a: Fight other táltos in a three-day spirit trance
d: Train with elder shamans for seven years
Defeat the lord of the forest in combat
Drink the blood of a sacred animal
Complete a pilgrimage across the mountains
Be struck by lightning and survive
e: Unlike most European magical figures, the táltos was not trained but born. Their initiation required a prolonged cataleptic trance during which they battled rival táltos while shape-shifted into bulls or stallions.

s: In Turkic mythology, the Yilbegan is a many-headed dragon that can only be killed by cutting off every head — but the last head always screams a prophecy before dying.
q: What multi-headed dragon from Turkic mythology screams a prophecy from its final head before dying?
e: Yilbegan stories span from Siberia to Central Asia. The creature typically has 7, 9, or 12 heads, and heroes must discover which head is the 'life head' that, once severed, kills the entire beast.

s: The Manananggal of Filipino folklore is a vampire-like creature that separates its upper torso from its lower body at night and flies around with bat wings hunting pregnant women.
q: How do you kill a Manananggal in Filipino folklore?
a: Sprinkle salt on its abandoned lower body
d: Drive a blessed silver spike through its heart
Expose it to sunlight at dawn
Cut off its head with a blessed sword
e: To kill a Manananggal, you must find the abandoned lower half and sprinkle it with salt, garlic, or ash — this prevents the torso from reattaching at dawn, destroying the creature.

s: Pan, the Greek god of shepherds, had such a terrifying battle cry that it caused irrational fear in enemies — which is where the English word 'panic' comes from.
q: Which Greek god's terrifying cry is the origin of the English word 'panic'?
e: During the Battle of Marathon (490 BCE), the Athenians credited Pan with causing the Persian army to flee in terror. The Greeks built him a shrine on the Acropolis in gratitude.

s: Hestia was so respected among the Greek gods that she voluntarily gave up her seat on Mount Olympus to Dionysus to avoid conflict — the only god to do so peacefully.
q: Which Greek goddess voluntarily gave up her Olympian seat to Dionysus?
e: As goddess of the hearth, Hestia valued harmony above status. Every Greek meal began and ended with an offering to her, and her sacred flame burned continuously in every city's public hall.

s: Hecate, the Greek goddess of crossroads and witchcraft, was the only Titan that Zeus allowed to keep her powers after the Olympians won the war against the Titans.
q: Which Titan goddess of witchcraft and crossroads kept her powers after the Olympians defeated the Titans?
e: Hecate predates the Olympians and was honored by Zeus because she sided with him during the Titanomachy. She held power over heaven, earth, and sea — making her one of the most powerful deities in the entire Greek pantheon.

s: Nike, the Greek goddess of victory, was depicted as winged — but the Athenians built a wingless statue of her so that victory could never fly away from their city.
q: Why did the Athenians build a wingless statue of Nike?
a: So victory could never fly away from Athens
d: To make her statue more durable
To prevent it from being stolen
To symbolize peace rather than war
e: The Temple of Athena Nike on the Acropolis contained the famous 'Apteros Nike' (Wingless Victory). This superstitious act shows how literally the ancient Greeks took their mythological symbolism.

s: Aeolus, the Greek keeper of the winds, gave Odysseus a bag containing all unfavorable winds — but his crew opened it thinking it was gold, blowing them back across the entire sea.
q: What happened when Odysseus's crew opened the bag of winds given by Aeolus?
a: They were blown back across the sea, away from home
d: All the winds escaped and created a terrible storm
The bag turned to lead and sank the ship
They were blown backwards through time
The winds released all their anger at once
His crew was turned to stone
e: Odysseus could literally see Ithaca on the horizon when his crew's greed undid him. Aeolus refused to help a second time, declaring that Odysseus must be cursed by the gods.

s: The Lilin in ancient Middle Eastern mythology were night demons born from Lilith — they attacked sleeping men and caused sleep paralysis, making them an ancient explanation for the phenomenon.
q: In ancient Middle Eastern mythology, what demons born from Lilith were believed to cause sleep paralysis?
e: The Lilin appear in Jewish, Babylonian, and Assyrian texts. Their connection to nocturnal terror made them one of the earliest cultural explanations for sleep paralysis, predating modern medicine by millennia.

s: The hippocamp — a horse with a fish tail — was Poseidon's chariot-puller in Greek mythology and inspired the name of the hippocampus brain region, which is shaped like a seahorse.
q: What mythological creature that pulled Poseidon's chariot inspired the name of a brain region?
e: Ancient Greek artists depicted Poseidon riding across the waves on a chariot drawn by hippocampi. When anatomists discovered a seahorse-shaped brain structure in the 16th century, they named it after this mythical creature.

s: Phobos and Deimos, the twin sons of Ares in Greek mythology, personified Fear and Dread — and they rode into battle on their father's chariot to paralyze enemies before combat even began.
q: In Greek mythology, what did Phobos and Deimos — the sons of Ares — personify?
e: Mars's two moons are named after these terrifying twins. In the Iliad, Phobos and Deimos accompanied Ares and the war goddess Enyo, spreading terror so effectively that armies would rout before a single blow was struck.

s: Zagreus, the first incarnation of Dionysus in Orphic mythology, was torn apart by the Titans as an infant — and humanity was created from the Titans' ashes mixed with his divine essence.
q: In Orphic mythology, what god was torn apart by Titans as a baby, with humans created from the mixed ashes?
e: This origin myth gave humans a dual nature: base Titanic matter and divine Dionysian spark. The Orphic mystery cults used this story to teach that humans contain a fragment of the divine that must be purified through reincarnation.

s: Adonis in Greek mythology was so beautiful that Aphrodite and Persephone fought over him — Zeus had to intervene, ruling he would spend four months with each goddess and four months alone.
q: Which two Greek goddesses fought over the beautiful Adonis, requiring Zeus to split his time between them?
e: Adonis was born from a myrrh tree after his mother was transformed by the gods. His annual death and rebirth became linked to the cycle of seasons, making him one of the earliest dying-and-rising gods in Western mythology.

s: The Bolla is an Albanian dragon that sleeps for the entire year with its eyes shut — if it sees a human on the one day it opens them, it must devour that person.
q: What Albanian dragon sleeps all year and must devour any human it sees when it opens its eyes?
d: Bolla emerges for one week and returns to sleep
Bolla transforms after ten years into a giant eagle
Bolla must eat seven humans to survive
Bolla spends half its year awake and half asleep
Bolla only wakes during full moons
Bolla's eyes must be kept closed with magic
e: On Saint George's Day, the Bolla opens its eyes. If it survives twelve years, it transforms into the far more powerful Kulshedra — a multi-headed dragon that causes droughts and demands human sacrifices.

s: In Filipino mythology, the Bakunawa is a sea serpent so massive it tried to swallow all seven moons — ancient Filipinos would bang pots and drums during lunar eclipses to scare it into spitting the moon back out.
q: In Filipino mythology, why did people bang pots and drums during lunar eclipses?
a: To scare the Bakunawa into spitting out the moon
d: To celebrate the moon god's birthday
To create rainbows in the sky
To punish the Bakunawa for being mischievous
e: Pre-colonial Filipino cosmology originally featured seven moons. The Bakunawa swallowed six, and the seventh survived only because humans made enough noise to frighten it. This noise-making tradition persisted for centuries.

s: The Leshy, a Slavic forest spirit, seals deals by sitting on a tree stump and removing its left shoe — any human who does the same and shakes its hand is bound to the bargain forever.
q: How does the Leshy — a Slavic forest spirit — seal a binding deal with a human?
a: Both sit on a stump with left shoes removed and shake hands
d: Exchange blood under a full moon
Share a meal of mushrooms and bread
Dance three times around a sacred tree
Exchange gifts of iron and silver
Sing a forest song in unison
e: The Leshy can appear as a tall man or shrink to the size of grass. Shepherds and hunters would make pacts with the Leshy for protection, but breaking the deal meant the forest itself would turn against you.

s: In Aztec mythology, the god Xolotl — twin brother of Quetzalcoatl — guided dead souls to the underworld and could transform into an axolotl salamander to hide from death himself.
q: Which Aztec god — twin brother of Quetzalcoatl — escaped death by shapeshifting into a salamander?
e: Xolotl was the god of lightning, fire, and deformity. When the gods sacrificed themselves to set the Fifth Sun in motion, Xolotl tried to escape death by shapeshifting — first into a corn stalk, then a maguey plant, and finally into the aquatic axolotl, which bears his name.

s: In Finnish mythology, the Sampo was a magical mill that produced infinite grain, salt, and gold — but when it shattered, its fragments sank to the sea floor and that is why seawater is salty.
q: In Finnish mythology, what magical device's shattered fragments explain why seawater is salty?
e: The Sampo is the central treasure of the Kalevala, Finland's national epic. The wizard Väinämöinen and the smith Ilmarinen fought the witch Louhi to reclaim it, but the battle destroyed the device and scattered its pieces into the ocean.

s: The Qilin in Chinese mythology is so gentle that it walks on clouds to avoid crushing a single blade of grass — yet its appearance signals the birth or death of a great sage.
q: What Chinese mythological creature walks on clouds to avoid crushing grass and heralded the birth of Confucius?
e: Often mistranslated as 'Chinese unicorn,' the Qilin has dragon scales, deer antlers, and an ox tail. Legend says one appeared before the birth of Confucius, carrying a jade tablet prophesying his greatness.

s: In Maori mythology, the demigod Maui died trying to achieve immortality by crawling into the sleeping goddess of death Hine-nui-te-po — a fantail bird laughed and woke her, and she crushed him.
q: In Maori mythology, what woke the goddess of death and caused Maui's fatal attempt at immortality to fail?
d: His wife Hine-titama called to him
A fish ate the goddess's hand
e: Maui had already slowed the sun and fished up the North Island of New Zealand. His plan was to enter Hine-nui-te-po's body and emerge from her mouth, reversing death. But the tiwakawaka (fantail) could not contain its laughter, dooming humanity to mortality.

s: The Pooka (Puca) in Irish folklore can shapeshift into any animal but always appears jet black — and it is the origin of the name 'Puck' from Shakespeare's A Midsummer Night's Dream.
q: What Irish shapeshifting creature inspired the character Puck in Shakespeare's A Midsummer Night's Dream?
e: The Pooka is neither fully good nor evil. It might give you a wild midnight ride as a horse or offer prophetic advice as a talking eagle. After November 1st (Samhain), all unharvested crops belong to the Pooka.
